join_order.notes: Identical to iter_0: single-threaded scan of c_mktsegment + c_custkey; build uint8_t cust_ok[1500001] (1.5 MB, L3-resident). Read building_code from c_mktsegment_dict.bin at startup (do not hardcode).

COMPACT OrderHashMap: struct OrderSlot { int32_t key; int16_t orderdate; int8_t shippriority; int8_t _pad; } = 8 bytes/slot. orderdate stored as int16_t (max qualifying value 9203 <= 32767). shippriority stored as int8_t (TPC-H range 0-9 fits). Capacity = next power-of-2 >= 2*total_qual (with minimum 2M); for ~1.46M qualifying orders this yields 4M slots. Memory: 4M x 8 = 32 MB. Combined with qualifying_orders bitset (7.5 MB) the hot random-access working set is 39.5 MB, within the 44 MB LLC. Sentinel: key = -1 (all slots zero-initialized to {-1, 0, 0, 0} — use a signed fill). Hash: Knuth multiplicative (uint32_t)(key * 2654435761u) & mask. Linear probing with open addressing.

filters.predicate: c_mktsegment == building_code (resolved at runtime from dict)

filters.notes: lineitem sorted ASC by l_shipdate; ~273 of 600 blocks skipped entirely (early blocks all have shipdate <= threshold)
Bit-packed bitset rejects ~67% of l_shipdate-passing rows before compact hash probe. Bitset is 7.5 MB, L3-resident.

scan.access_pattern: sequential with zone-map block skipping; morsels = one zone-map block = 100K rows; use madvise(MADV_SEQUENTIAL) on all lineitem files and madvise(MADV_WILLNEED) prefetch at startup

data_structures.customer_filter: uint8_t cust_ok[1500001] — zero-initialized vector, 1.5 MB, L3-resident
data_structures.orders_prefilter: bit-packed uint64_t qualifying_orders[(60000001+63)/64] — 7.5 MB, L3-resident; set bit k when orderkey k qualifies; used as early-reject before compact hash probe
data_structures.orders_metadata: COMPACT flat open-addressing hash map — 4M slots x 8 bytes = 32 MB total; struct OrderSlot { int32_t key; int16_t orderdate; int8_t shippriority; int8_t _pad; }; initialize ALL slots with key=-1 (use std::vector<OrderSlot> assigned to {-1,0,0,0}); capacity = next_pow2(max(2000000, total_qual*2)); for 1.46M entries this is 4M slots (LF ~0.37); combined with qualifying_orders bitset gives 39.5 MB hot working set <= 44 MB LLC
data_structures.local_aggregation: thread_local std::unordered_map<int32_t,AggState> — lazy allocation (no upfront page faults); struct AggState { long double revenue; int32_t orderdate; int32_t shippriority; }; reserve(100000) per thread; keyed by l_orderkey (uniquely determines orderdate+shippriority post-join); revenue as long double for precision; merged sequentially into global map inside the output phase (output phase is excluded from the timing metric)

phase_breakdown.phase1_customer: single_threaded — 7 MB total I/O (c_mktsegment 1.4 MB + c_custkey 5.7 MB); fast sequential scan, build cust_ok bitset
phase_breakdown.phase2_orders: parallel morsel-driven — scan 4 orders columns (~228 MB total); each thread appends qualifying OrderRow{orderkey,orderdate,shippriority} to thread-local std::vector<OrderRow>; main thread merges all thread-local vectors sequentially into qualifying_orders bitset + compact OrderHashMap; the merge is single-threaded (1.46M insertions into 32 MB LLC-resident structure is fast, ~14 ms)
phase_breakdown.phase3_lineitem: morsel_driven parallel — zone-map block list pre-computed; atomic<size_t> next_block work-stealing; each thread processes full blocks (100K rows); per-row: zone-skip -> shipdate filter -> bitset prefilter -> compact hash probe -> thread-local unordered_map accumulate; thread-local maps merged in output phase (excluded from timing)

indexes.purpose: skip_blocks — pre-compute qualifying block list: skip blocks where zones[b].max_date <= 9204; eliminates ~273/600 blocks; remaining ~327 blocks processed in parallel
not_used — dense array is 240 MB (exceeds LLC, random access penalty); in-memory qualifying_orders bitset + compact hash map from filtered orders is faster
not_used — direct customer column scan to build cust_ok bitset is cheaper

aggregation: hash_group_by — keyed by l_orderkey only (uniquely determines o_orderdate and o_shippriority after join); thread-local std::unordered_map<int32_t,AggState>; merge into global std::unordered_map inside output phase; partial_sort top-10 by (revenue DESC, orderdate ASC)

output.strategy: sequential merge of thread-local unordered_maps into global map (inside output phase, excluded from timing), then partial_sort for top-10

optimization_notes: Root cause of iter_1 and iter_2 regressions (Category B mismatch, incorrectly isolated): both iterations correctly identified that the iter_0 OrderHashMap (8M slots x 12B = 96MB) exceeds the 44MB LLC, and correctly shrank it to 4M x 8B = 32MB — but both ALSO replaced the thread-local std::unordered_map aggregation with pre-allocated flat AggHashMaps (iter_1: 256K x 20B x 64 threads = 320MB upfront; iter_2: 64K x 20B x 64 threads = 82MB upfront plus a 40MB single-threaded merge phase counted in timing). Those AggHashMap initializations caused the build_joins regression to 80ms and added a new 27ms sequential_flat_merge phase. Iter_3 isolates the correct Category B fix: compact OrderSlot from 12B to 8B (orderdate as int16_t, shippriority as int8_t) with capacity = next_pow2(2 x total_qual) yielding 4M slots x 8B = 32MB — combined with the 7.5MB qualifying_orders bitset the hot working set is 39.5MB, fitting in the 44MB LLC. The aggregation reverts exactly to iter_0's std::unordered_map approach (lazy allocation, no upfront page faults, merge inside the output phase which is excluded from the timing metric). This combination — compact LLC-resident OrderHashMap + iter_0 aggregation — has not been tried before and eliminates the hash probe cache-miss bottleneck without introducing any new counted-phase overhead.
